title: Adjacency List
description: Each vertex keeps just its own neighbors: O(V+E) space, the far more common representation for sparse graphs and graph traversal.
objectives: Build an adjacency list for a graph
Know that edge checks cost O(degree), not O(1)
Compare adjacency list against adjacency matrix directly

sections.text: Graph Representation previewed the list as "for each vertex, a list of exactly the vertices it directly connects to." This is the representation you'll see far more often in practice, especially for BFS/Graph BFS and DFS/Graph DFS coming up next.
Recall push_back() from vector. adj[i] only ever stores i's actual neighbors — no wasted entries for pairs that aren't connected, unlike the matrix's full grid.
Compare directly to Adjacency Matrix's grid for the same graph: instead of a full 4×4 table with mostly zeros, each vertex's row only lists exactly who it's actually connected to.

Total space is proportional to the number of vertices (V, for the outer array) plus the total number of edges (E, for all the neighbor entries combined) — recall Space Complexity. For a sparse graph, where E is much smaller than V², this is dramatically less than the matrix's fixed O(V²).

This is the trade-off: checking "does an edge exist between vertex A and vertex B" with an adjacency list means scanning through A's neighbor list — O(degree of A) in the worst case, not the matrix's guaranteed O(1). For graph traversal (visiting every neighbor of a vertex, which BFS and DFS do constantly), this is exactly what's needed anyway — an adjacency list gives you a vertex's neighbors directly, without wasting time scanning a whole row of mostly zeros.

rows: O(V+E) space — efficient for sparse graphs
Iterating a vertex's neighbors wastes time on non-edges
Iterating a vertex's neighbors is direct and efficient
Better for sparse graphs — the far more common real-world case

sections.title: Each person's own contact list, instead of a village-wide grid
sections.text: Recall the matrix's round-robin tournament grid. Most real social networks are more like everyone just keeping their own personal contact list — Aman's list has his actual friends, not a full village-wide grid marking yes/no against every single other resident. For a sparse network like that, personal lists are dramatically more efficient than one giant grid.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to add both directions for an undirected edge — adj[0].push_back(1); alone only records the connection from 0's side.
Assuming edge existence checks are O(1) with an adjacency list — they're O(degree), since the neighbor list must be scanned.
Using an adjacency matrix for a large, sparse graph out of habit — the O(V²) cost is usually unjustified there.
Forgetting adjacency list is the standard choice for traversal-heavy algorithms like BFS and DFS, where visiting each neighbor directly is exactly what's needed.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does an adjacency list represent a graph?
questions.answer: Each vertex stores a list of exactly the vertices it directly connects to, rather than a full grid marking every possible pair.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of an adjacency list, and how does it compare to a matrix's?
questions.answer: O(V+E), proportional to vertices plus actual edges — far better than a matrix's fixed O(V²) for a sparse graph.

questions.question: Why is edge-existence checking slower with an adjacency list than a matrix?
questions.answer: Because it requires scanning through a vertex's neighbor list, O(degree) in the worst case, rather than a matrix's direct O(1) index lookup.

questions.question: Why is adjacency list generally preferred for graph traversal algorithms like BFS and DFS?
questions.answer: Because traversal needs to visit each vertex's actual neighbors directly and efficiently, which an adjacency list provides without wasting time scanning a full row of mostly non-existent edges, as a matrix would.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An adjacency list represents a graph by storing, for each vertex, a list of just its actual neighbors — adj[i].push_back(neighbor). This costs O(V+E) space, proportional to vertices plus actual edges, dramatically better than a matrix's fixed O(V²) for sparse graphs, which are far more common in practice. The trade-off is that checking whether a specific edge exists costs O(degree), scanning a neighbor list, rather than a matrix's O(1) lookup — but for traversal algorithms like BFS and DFS, which need to visit every neighbor of a vertex anyway, an adjacency list is both more space-efficient and a more natural fit.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Adjacency List
sections.text: Adjacency list = everyone's own personal contact list, not a village-wide grid. O(V+E) space, efficient for sparse graphs. The standard choice for BFS/DFS traversal.
